ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dried lotus seed as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for this ingredient is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried lotus seeds is approximately 30 g (about a small handful), commonly used in soups, dessert broths, or consumed as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Lotus Seeds, Dried — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for dried lotus seeds (cr_id F09030027). Saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16056 (Lotus seeds, raw) where HPB does not report those fields, with appropriate scaling noted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on dried legume/seed category (boiled lotus seeds GI ~54 used as closest defensible analogue for dried lotus seeds)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried lotus seeds was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 54 is derived from the closest defensible analogue: lotus seeds (boiled/cooked) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Dried lotus seeds are typically rehydrated and cooked before consumption, making this analogue appropriate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 64.47 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 10.4 per 30 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (1368 mg/100 g) providing a strongly favourable Na:K ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Dried lotus seeds are suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions (30–50 g dry weight); avoid sweetened dessert preparations with added salt or sugar that would undermine the cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (64.47 g/100 g) with moderate dietary fibre (5.6 g/100 g); low GI (~54) requires portion control to manage postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit dried lotus seed portions to 20–30 g dry weight per serving, pair with protein-rich foods to blunt glycaemic response, and strictly avoid sweetened lotus seed preparations such as lotus paste in mooncakes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, high potassium (1368 mg/100 g), and meaningful dietary fibre (5.6 g/100 g) collectively support a cardioprotective nutritional profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried lotus seeds are a heart-friendly plant protein source suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; consume in moderate portions as part of a balanced diet and avoid high-sugar or high-sodium preparation methods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat, no trans fat, meaningful dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health and immune modulation; calcium and potassium support tissue health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried lotus seeds are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; pair with omega-3-rich foods such as fatty fish or walnuts for stronger anti-inflammatory benefit, and avoid sweetened preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried lotus seeds (莲子, Lian Zi) are the mature, dehusked, and dehydrated seeds of the sacred lotus plant (Nelumbo nucifera), widely used across Chinese, Southeast Asian, and South Asian culinary traditions. They are pale yellow to cream in colour, with a firm, dense texture when dry that softens to a mealy, starchy consistency upon rehydration and cooking. Their flavour is mild, subtly sweet, and slightly earthy. Nutritionally, dried lotus seeds are a high-protein (15.4 g/100 g), high-carbohydrate (64.5 g/100 g) plant food with very low fat (1.97 g/100 g) and negligible sodium (5 mg/100 g). They are exceptionally rich in potassium (1368 mg/100 g), making them strongly aligned with DASH dietary principles for blood pressure management. Calcium (163 mg/100 g) and iron (3.53 mg/100 g) are also notable. Dietary fibre is approximately 5.6 g/100 g. In traditional Chinese medicine, lotus seeds are valued for their calming and digestive properties. Culinary uses include sweet dessert soups (tong sui), lotus seed paste for mooncakes and pastries, savoury braised dishes, and herbal broths. They are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. The low glycemic index (~54) means portion control is important for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, they are suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to their exceptional potassium content and low saturated fat profile, but require moderation for Type 2 Diabetes due to high carbohydrate density.
common_user_queries: What are lotus seeds used for in Chinese cooking?
Are lotus seeds high in protein?
What is the glycemic index of lotus seeds?
Are lotus seeds good for blood pressure?
How do you cook dried lotus seeds?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09030027, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, dietary fibre, sugar, and B-vitamin values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16056 (Lotus seeds, raw) which is an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed international GI table (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) as no direct dried lotus seed GI entry exists in the University of Sydney database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.49 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.91 g/100 g), dietary fibre (5.6 g/100 g), sugar (0.0 g/100 g confirmed — lotus seeds contain negligible free sugars as the carbohydrate is predominantly starch), B-vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6), phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and water content supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16056 (Lotus seeds, raw) as HPB does not report these fields. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for dried lotus seeds as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: lotus seeds are a whole plant food with no industrial trans fat; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central NDB 16056. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero: lotus seeds are a plant-derived food and contain no cholesterol. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: plant-derived food with no B12. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: no significant vitamin D in plant seeds. 7. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on lotus seeds (boiled) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (AJCN); no direct dried lotus seed GI entry found in University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code represent best available matches; SNOMED CT coverage for this food item is absent from the standard clinical terminology as of 2026-04-16.
